notes: WIN-SHL-002. Presence-only pins of the REAL, pre-existing, non-Material toolbar-overflow reachability/order mechanism that docs/design/01-foundations.md 7 and docs/design/05-navigation.md 1.3/4.3 rely on when they promise 'every command remains reachable (overflow is a designed state, never silent removal); keyboard order is stable across reflows' and 'arrows traverse commands in stable declared order'. A clipped toolbar item stays mbVisible=true (only its rect empties, distinguishing overflow from removal); both the native custom-menu builder and the framework floating overflow toolbar walk items in original declared order; and arrow-key highlight cycling folds the '>>' menu button into the sequence at both wrap points. IMPORTANT HONESTY BOUND: this contract MUST NOT be reported as advancing the row's M (Material-specific) gate. The inventory legend states 'Existing upstream UI source does not satisfy M', and none of this code is Material-specific -- there is no VCL_FILE_WIDGET_THEME guard and no definition.xml token for the overflow affordance (definition.xml has no <toolbar> MenuButton/chevron part). This code predates and is independent of the Material redesign; the contract only proves the design chapter's reachability/order promise is grounded in real, non-vaporware code today, exactly like the WIN-NAV-002 context_menu presence-marker precedent. Scope excludes: any new Material visual composition for the overflow chevron/menu-button (none specified anywhere today), and the three compact/medium/expanded window-width classes from 01-foundations.md 7 (unimplemented, owned by the separate row WIN-FND-006, not this one). Source evidence only: no Windows build, toolbar pixels, or runtime resize/keyboard interaction are claimed (runtime_verified false).

code_markers.why: A clipped item stays mbVisible=true with only an empty rect -- overflow is not removal (IsItemHidden is the separate !mbVisible case).
The per-item clipped query the framework overflow builder consumes.
The std::any_of gate that reports whether an overflow affordance is needed at all.

code_markers.pattern: ToolBox::UpdateCustomMenu\(PopupMenu\* pMenu\).*?for \( const auto& rItem : mpData->m_aItems \).*?if\( rItem\.IsClipped\(\) \).*?pMenu->InsertItem\(.*?pMenu->InsertSeparator\(\);.*?if\( rItem\.IsItemHidden\(\) \)
code_markers.why: Ascending walk over m_aItems inserts the clipped group in declared order, one separator, then the hidden group -- never reordered or dropped. (The source comment 'in the order they would usually appear in the toolbar' is stripped before matching; the loop structure is what is pinned.)
Arrow-key highlight cycling folds the '>>' menu button into the sequence at a wrap point, so keyboard traversal reaches overflow in stable order rather than skipping it.
The '>>' affordance paints only when items are actually clipped (a designed state, not always-on chrome).
The vcl-to-framework glue that joins the native menu-button to the framework overflow handler -- the actual triad joint.

code_markers.pattern: ToolBarManager::FillOverflowToolbar\(.*?for \( ToolBox::ImplToolItems::size_type i = 0; i < pParent->GetItemCount\(\); \+\+i \).*?if \( pParent->IsItemClipped\( nId \) \).*?m_pToolBar->InsertItem\( nId,
code_markers.why: The floating overflow popup toolbar independently walks ascending indices, tests IsItemClipped, and re-inserts in that order -- preserving declared order a second, independent way.
